ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Burmese spicy chicken soup; closest parent 'chicken soup food product' (FOODON_00002700) used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Chicken soup) is the closest defensible parent; no Burmese-specific spicy chicken soup entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Burmese spicy chicken soup; SNOMED CT does not catalogue ethnic-specific soup preparations at this granularity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Burmese spicy chicken soup, approximately 350 g, as typically served at a Myanmar restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken soup, home-prepared (USDA SR Legacy, NDB No. 06172) adapted with spice-profile adjustment for Burmese-style preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Burmese spicy chicken soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. USDA SR Legacy chicken soup (home-prepared, NDB 06172 / FDC 172938) was used as the closest defensible analogue. Values for energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate are taken directly from the USDA entry per 100 g. Sodium is elevated slightly to reflect fish sauce and additional seasoning typical of Burmese-style preparation, estimated at 480 mg per 100 g based on USDA data for chicken soup with added salt and fish sauce seasoning. Saturated fat, dietary fibre, and sugar are adapted from the same USDA source. All adaptations are conservative and documented.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; chicken-based broth soups analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese spicy chicken soup. GI estimated at 30 based on analogous low-GI chicken-based soups and broths documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables. Chicken soups with low carbohydrate content (3–5 g per 100 g) consistently fall in the low-GI range (GI 25–40). GL per serving calculated as (30 × 3.2 × 350) / (100 × 100) = 3.36, rounded to 3.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and seasoning (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume this soup with caution due to its high sodium content from fish sauce and added salt; a single serving may contribute approximately 70–75% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg, and portion reduction or requesting a low-sodium preparation is strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (3.2 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~30) resulting in a low glycemic load per serving (~3.4)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken spicy soup is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index; the high protein content from chicken supports satiety and glycaemic stability, though sodium from fish sauce should be monitored in those with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium content raises cardiovascular risk; fat content is moderate with a reasonable saturated fat level
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1680 mg per serving) is the primary cardiovascular concern for this dish; the relatively low saturated fat content (0.7 g per 100 g) and lean chicken protein are favourable, but individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium preparation to manage blood pressure and fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Spicy Soup (ကြက်သောက်ဆမ်း, romanized: Kyat Thoke Hsam) is a traditional Burmese soup dish made by simmering chicken pieces in a spiced broth flavoured with chilli, lemongrass, ginger, garlic, onion, turmeric, and fish sauce. The dish is a staple of Myanmar cuisine and is served at Myanmar restaurants in Singapore, including at Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant. The soup has a clear to lightly cloudy broth with a pronounced spicy and aromatic character, a savoury umami depth from fish sauce, and a warming heat from fresh or dried chilli. The texture is light and brothy with tender chicken pieces. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy, high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish (approximately 55 kcal, 4.8 g protein, 2.5 g fat, and 3.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic-friendly dietary patterns. The primary clinical concern is its sodium content, which is elevated due to fish sauce and seasoning, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 30 (low), and the glycemic load per standard 350 g bowl is approximately 3.4, confirming its suitability for blood glucose management. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is typically halal when prepared with halal-certified chicken and fish sauce.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Burmese spicy chicken soup exists in any primary regional approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy chicken soup (FDC 172938) with documented adjustments for Burmese-style seasoning. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database category for low-carbohydrate chicken soups. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC 172938 (chicken soup, home-prepared) with sodium elevated to 480 mg per 100 g to reflect fish sauce and additional Burmese-style seasoning; all adaptations are conservative and documented. 2. Vitamin A, B-vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12), folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and selenium are set to -1 as these micronutrients are not reported in the adapted USDA SR Legacy source entry used for this estimation and cannot be reliably extrapolated without a direct laboratory analysis of the Burmese preparation. 3. Trans fat is 0.0 g per 100 g; confirmed as negligible/trace in home-prepared chicken soups per USDA SR Legacy data. 4. GI is estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous chicken-based broth soups from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Burmese spicy chicken soup was found in peer-reviewed literature. 5. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON included 'Noodle' and 'Lor Mee', which appear to be erroneous tags from the field survey platform data aggregation; this record has been classified as a soup dish, not a noodle or Lor Mee dish, based on the food name and Burmese culinary context. 6. The food is served at Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant, 77 High Street, Singapore, as sourced from field survey data at SGD 8.90. 7. Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided following Singaporean multilingual naming rules, as this dish is served in Singapore. Burmese script (ကြက်သောက်ဆမ်း) is preserved in romanized_local and aliases.
